mnemonics.title: Dynamic Array Growth Rule
mnemonics.content: D O U B L E = Double capacity when Overflow Upsizes: Bloat is Low, Expand (0.75 threshold). USE 2X GROWTH, 0.25 SHRINK THRESHOLD.

mnemonics.title: Time Complexities
mnemonics.content: A C E = Access by index (Constant), Copy all for resize (n), Everything else may shift (n).

mnemonics.title: Dynamic Array Characteristics
mnemonics.content: C A C H E = Contiguous memory, Amortized O(1), Cache-friendly, High random access, Expensive mid-inserts

acronyms.usage: Stack operations use dynamic array for push/pop at end in O(1)
Queue implementations can use dynamic array with two pointers for O(1) operations
C++ STL provides vector as dynamic array implementation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a rectangular container that doubles in width (2x, 4x, 8x...) when it fills up. Elements are copied to the new larger container, leaving space for future additions.
Think of a water tank. When water level (elements) exceeds 75% of tank capacity, tank automatically expands. When it drops below 25%, tank shrinks to save space.
Draw houses in a row on a street. Each house (element) has an address (index). CPU can quickly visit any house by calculating its address, unlike houses scattered around a city (linked list).

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant that adds more tables when 75% are occupied. When it adds tables, all customers must move to the bigger section (copying elements), but future customers fit easily.
Dynamic array is like a parking lot with numbered spaces (direct access). Linked list is like street parking where each car knows where the next one is parked.
Buying a membership card: paying $100 once gives you 100 free coffees. Each coffee costs $1 on average (amortized), even though the actual transaction was $100 for many at once.
